resume:
Curriculum & Instruction Specialist — Middle School Mathematics

Profile: Six years designing and implementing math curriculum and supporting teachers on data-driven instruction across a mid-size district.

Key results (STAR):
- Situation: 6th-grade math proficiency stalled at 41% on the state assessment. Task: lift proficiency without adding instructional minutes. Action: rebuilt the pacing guide around three high-leverage standards and coached teachers on a weekly formative-assessment routine. Result: proficiency rose to 56% over two school years across nine schools.
- Situation: New teachers churning in year one. Task: improve retention and ramp. Action: built a structured mentor program with biweekly co-planning and classroom visits. Result: first-year retention improved from 68% to 84%.
- Situation: Inconsistent intervention for struggling students. Task: standardize tier-2 support. Action: designed a small-group intervention block with progress monitoring every two weeks. Result: median growth for tier-2 students doubled versus the prior cohort.

Experience:
Curriculum Specialist, Math — Fairmont Unified School District (2021-present)
Instructional Coach — Fairmont USD (2019-2021)
Middle School Math Teacher — Cedar Hollow Schools (2017-2019)

Education: MEd Curriculum & Instruction; BA Mathematics. State teaching license, math endorsement.
jd: Brookline Public Schools seeks a Curriculum & Instruction Specialist for secondary mathematics. Reporting to the Director of Teaching & Learning, you will own the 6-8 math scope and sequence, lead job-embedded coaching for ~40 teachers across six schools, and drive measurable gains on interim and state assessments. You will design the formative-assessment cadence, run the tier-2 intervention framework, and use data to target coaching. We want a specialist who answers questions precisely and concisely, stays on the question asked, and can tie a specific instructional move to a specific proficiency number. Strong classroom credibility and a track record of moving proficiency are required.

history.q: Tell me about your current role and the math outcomes you're accountable for.
You moved 6th-grade proficiency from 41% to 56%. What was the single most important instructional change you made?
Keep it tight: of that 15-point gain, how much do you attribute to the pacing-guide change versus the formative-assessment routine?

candidate_last_answer: Great question, and honestly it connects to something I feel really strongly about in education more broadly. You know, when I started teaching back in Cedar Hollow, the whole system was so different — we didn't even have a real pacing guide, and that got me thinking about how districts underinvest in instructional infrastructure, which is a whole soapbox of mine. And it reminds me of this conference I went to last spring, the keynote was about productive struggle and growth mindset, which I've been bringing into our PD sessions, and that ties into the mentor program too because new teachers really need that culture. Oh, and we also rolled out a new family-engagement night that I'm super proud of. So there's a lot going on, and I think it all kind of works together to move kids — it's hard to pull just one thread out, you know?

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: it's hard to pull just one thread out

expected-yield: a decomposition of the 15-point proficiency gain into the pacing-guide change versus the formative-assessment routine, with an approximate share or point-estimate for each, forcing the candidate off the conference/family-night tangents back to the specific attribution asked
